So first I am going to feed you the advice and then I am going to feed you my CV which I would love for you to build up from scratch.

Absolutely, I can guide you on how to optimize your CV for ATS systems and entry-level positions in marketing, sales, and business development roles. Let's start with the advice you received, and then we can proceed to review and improve your CV. Please share the advice and the details of your current CV when you're ready.

This is the advice, in the next message I will give you my CV, I am also applying for other roles too so try to keep it somewhat general as well, balance is key! "
Hi Qasim,

This free CV critique is intended to give you an honest, straightforward assessment and some suggestions to help in your job search. I'm able to offer insight into how you compare to other job seekers competing for the same positions because I personally review hundreds of CVs each month.

CV Writing

Your career summary is not as long as it could be. This is a key piece to compel the hiring manager to keep reading. Flesh out your career summary to define you as a professional and cover those areas most relevant to your career level and job target.

Based on how your CV is worded, you come across as a "doer," rather than an "achiever." A few too many of your job descriptions are task-based rather than results-based. This means that they tell you what you did, instead of what you achieved. This is a normal mistake for non-professional CV writers. To inspire and generate intrigue, a great CV aids the hiring executive envisage you delivering similar achievements at his or her company. Here are some examples of task-based sentences in your CV:

    “Produced and edited high-Juality videos and photos for promotional use, English contributing to a consistent and engaging brand image”
    “Managed multi-platform online presence (Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Copywriting YouTube), increasing brand visibility and audience engagement”


Employers want to learn about your previous achievements and specifically how you've helped make an impact. More importantly, they want to know how you are going to make a significant difference at their organisation.

In reading your CV, I did not find the kind of active language that would bring your work to life. Instead, I saw passive words and non-action verbs.

Phrases like “Managed” and “Assisted in” are monotonous, overused, and add little value to your CV. Strong action verbs, used with engaging language to outline outstanding achievements, are essential parts of a soundly-built CV.

Now, let's see how to put this into practice. Here's a real life sample taken from a former client's CV. By changing the language, we helped to enhance the perception of the job-seeker.

    Passive language / Doing: “Negotiated contracts with vendors”
    Action language / Achieving: “Slashed payroll/benefits administration costs 30% by negotiating pricing and fees, while ensuring the continuation and enhancements of services.”


An adjustment like this makes a big improvement.

A regular review of every word and sentence in your CV is an important thing to do, especially if you're the only one looking at it. Hiring managers are looking for an excuse to eliminate you as a candidate. You may not be able to see awkward phrases and grammatical errors if you've already spent too much time with your own CV.

Digital Readiness

Your CV is saved in PDF format. 19% of CVs look like yours. While this may make viewing consistent for recruiters, some older Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) might not be able to correctly parse this. You might want to consider converting your document to a Microsoft Word file.
CV Scan Test Results Hiring Managers use applicant tracking systems (ATS) to automatically designate a Best Fit based on your experience level and the kinds of keywords spread throughout your CV. The Applicant Tracking System (ATS) will also assign a weighted score to Key Skills & Competencies from your CV to paint a picture of who you are as a potential employee.

Here is how your CV stacks up...
Where the Applicant Tracking System (ATS) thinks you fit:

Qasim, for a professional with 2 years of experience, your CV undersells you. This is the most important tool in your job search and it needs to clearly communicate your expertise, unique experience, accomplishments, and career goals.

Here are the major issues holding your CV back:

Show the outcomes of your efforts. Your CV focuses too much on what you’ve done, not what you’ve achieved.

Your CV does not make a strong visual impression. Your CV should appear clean, uncluttered, and appealing to the eye.

Your CV needs to include more keywords and skills to help position you as a stronger candidate."

93% of all Hiring Managers use CV scanning software called applicant tracking systems (ATS) to filter candidates from the application pool. To illustrate how you stand up to the automation, I passed your CV through the very same software that Hiring Managers use to filter the real talent from the hundreds, sometimes thousands, of candidates that apply for a single open position.

Thank you for providing the in-depth CV critique you received. Although I can't build an entire CV from scratch without specific information about your experience and qualifications, I can certainly offer tailored advice using the feedback you've been given to ensure your CV stands out, particularly for an entry-level candidate targeting marketing, sales, and business development roles.

Here are several key takeaways and suggestions based on the advice you received:

**1. Career Summary Improvement:**
   - Expand the career summary to give a clear picture of who you are as a professional. Highlight your passion for the field, relevant skills, internships, and any relevant projects or coursework.
  
**2. Highlight Achievements Over Tasks:**
   - Instead of simply listing the tasks you completed, focus on the outcomes and achievements. Quantify your results whenever possible. For example, instead of saying you "managed multi-platform online presence," say "Grew Instagram following by 30% within 6 months through targeted content strategies."

**3. Active Language:**
   - Use strong action verbs to bring your experiences to life. Replace generic phrases like “Managed” and “Assisted in” with more dynamic verbs such as "Orchestrated," "Spearheaded," "Amplified," etc.

**4. ATS-Friendly Formatting:**
   - While PDFs maintain consistent formatting, they might not always be parsed correctly by older ATS systems. Consider using a simple Word document and an ATS-friendly template.
   - Ensure you have a good balance of keywords from the job description and common industry terms, without keyword stuffing.

**5. Reflect the Correct Skillset:**
   - Tailor the skills section to include key skills relevant to the jobs you're applying for. If “operations” and “staff training” are not central to the roles you want, replace them with skills like “market analysis,” “social media management," or "lead generation."
   - Review the job postings in your desired field and incorporate their language and desirable skills into your CV.

**6. Visual Impression:**
   - Your CV should be neat, with a clear layout and ample white space. Use bullet points for readability and consistent formatting throughout the document.

**7. Include Measurable Outcomes:**
   - Emphasize how your actions benefited past projects or employers. For example, mention how your "video editing skills increased engagement by 20%" or how you "improved customer satisfaction by implementing a new feedback system."

To truly customize your CV and leverage the advice provided, I would need specific details about your educational background, work experiences, skills, and any certificates or accolades. Feel free to share that next, and I can help you with more directed suggestions.

This is some personal information but in my personal life I have done 1000s of hours of meditation, read 100s of books gone to spiritual and meditation retreats, is any of that relevant at all?

In general, personal achievements such as extensive meditation practice, reading extensively, and attending spiritual/meditation retreats are usually not included in traditional CVs or resumes, especially if they do not relate directly to the job you're applying for. The focus of your CV should be on your professional qualifications and work-related skills.

However, there are exceptions depending on the nature of the job or the company culture. If you are applying to companies that value personal development, wellness, and mindfulness as part of their mission and culture, mentioning these practices can be an asset. It shows commitment to self-improvement and discipline, qualities which can be beneficial in any role.

Here's how you might include such personal achievements in your CV, while maintaining professionalism:

**Activities & Interests**
- Deeply engaged in personal development through extensive meditation practice, accruing over 1,000 hours of dedicated mindfulness training.
- Avid reader with a passion for continuous learning, having explored a diverse range of literature spanning various topics and genres.
- Actively participate in spiritual and meditation retreats to enhance personal well-being and professional focus.

Mentioning these personal activities can differentiate you from other candidates and give you additional talking points during an interview. Just ensure these details are concise and placed at the end of your CV, under an "Activities & Interests" or "Personal Interests" section, so they don't overshadow your professional qualifications. Remember, balance is key and relevance to the job should be the guiding principle when deciding whether to include personal achievements or experiences on your CV.

One more thing, I am entry level and due to many personal reasons, I did not stay in jobs for long.. will this be a problem?

Frequent job changes or short stints at jobs can be a concern for some employers, as they may question an applicant's commitment or fit for a long-term role. However, this can be mitigated, especially for entry-level candidates, where some job-hopping is more common due to internships, temporary positions, or the search for the right career path.

Here are a few strategies that can help address short job durations in your CV or during interviews:

1. **Highlight Learning and Skills**: Emphasize the skills and experiences you gained at each position, focusing on how each role contributed to your professional growth and prepared you for the next step in your career.

2. **Frame It Positively**: Instead of focusing on the duration, make sure to describe your job changes positively. You can indicate that you were seeking opportunities for growth, new challenges, or a better fit for your career goals.

3. **Group Similar Experiences**: If you've done several internships or short-term positions that were similar, consider grouping them under one section titled “Internship Experience” or "Contract Positions" to draw attention to the cumulative experience rather than the duration of each.

4. **Be Ready to Explain**: If asked in an interview, be honest but positive about why your jobs were short-lived. Personal reasons can be delicate, so you do not have to provide details. A simple explanation that focuses on your future aspirations can suffice: "I'm looking for a role that aligns with my career goals and where I can contribute and grow long-term."

5. **Research Fit**: Apply for positions where there is a good fit with your skills and interests. Employers are more understanding of short job durations if they feel confident that you are genuinely interested in the role and the company.

6. **Network**: Sometimes a reference can overshadow a spotty job history. If someone can vouch for you, short job durations may be overlooked in favor of a personal recommendation.

7. **Consider the Format**: When listing positions on your CV, you can choose to list just years rather than months and years. This is common practice and can make short-term jobs seem less abrupt.

Being entry-level, you're not expected to have a long work history, so moving through a few roles whilst finding your feet can often be overlooked, especially when it's clear you have a strong academic background and relevant skills. Make sure that your CV conveys a narrative of progression, and remember that your enthusiasm, potential, and fit with the company culture are often as important as your work history.

do you think mentioning  medtition is weird?

Including meditation on your CV is not inherently weird, especially as mindfulness and personal well-being are increasingly recognized for their importance in professional environments. However, whether it's appropriate largely depends on the culture of the industry and the company you are applying to, as well as how you present it.

Consider the following points:

1. **Company Culture**: If you're applying to a company that values work-life balance, employee well-being, or has a modern, holistic approach to their work environment, mentioning your dedication to meditation can actually be seen as a positive attribute.

2. **Relevance to the Role**: If the role you are applying for involves stress management, creativity, focus, or interpersonal relations, mentioning meditation could be relevant as it demonstrates your proactive approach to developing these skills.

3. **Professionalism**: It's important to frame it in a professional manner. For example, indicating that meditation has helped you develop focus, resilience, or emotional intelligence can be beneficial.

If you choose to include it, place it in an "Activities & Interests" or "Personal Interests" section towards the end of your CV, and keep the mention brief. Here's how you might present it:

**Activities & Interests**
- Engage in daily meditation, enhancing focus, discipline, and stress management—skills I leverage to maintain high productivity and professional clarity.

Ultimately, it's a personal choice based on your understanding of the company and how integral you feel meditation is to who you are as a professional. If you believe it enhances your application and presents you as a more well-rounded candidate, then it could indeed make your CV stand out in a positive way. If you feel uncertain, it might be safer to leave it out or to be ready to discuss its professional benefits in an interview setting rather than on paper.

should the cv be optimised for each individual job?

Yes, ideally, your CV should be optimized for each individual job application. While it can be time-consuming, tailoring your CV for a specific role enhances your chances of getting noticed and ultimately getting an interview. Here's why and how you should optimize your CV for each job:

1. **Keywords**: Applicant tracking systems (ATS) filter CVs based on specific keywords related to the job description. Ensure that your CV includes relevant keywords and phrases from the job posting.

2. **Relevance**: Hiring managers want to see how your experience directly relates to the job they are looking to fill. Highlight your experience and skills that are most relevant to the role. For instance, if the job requires specific knowledge of online sales strategies and you have this experience, make sure it is prominent on your CV.

3. **Show Understanding**: By tailoring your CV to each job, you demonstrate that you understand what the role entails and that you have deliberately presented yourself as the right fit for that specific position.

4. **Stand Out**: A personalized CV can help you stand out from candidates who submit generic or broadly targeted CVs.

Here's how to optimize your CV for each application:

- **Study the Job Description**: Identify the skills, experience, and qualifications the employer is seeking. Note the language and keywords used.
  
- **Emphasize Relevant Experience**: Align your professional summary, work history, and skills section with the attributes the employer is looking for.

- **Quantify Achievements**: Use numbers to demonstrate your accomplishments when relevant to the role.

- **Adjust the Professional Summary**: Each time you apply for a new role, tweak your professional summary to match the objectives and duties of the position.

- **Match the Language**: Use similar language as the job posting to describe your experiences and responsibilities. This is particularly important for navigating ATS software.

- **Highlight Transferable Skills**: If you lack direct experience, focus on transferable skills that can be applicable to the position.

Always remember that while it's important to tailor your CV to the job, your adjustments should remain truthful to your experience and qualifications. Authenticity is key in your CV and during the interview process.
the next question is this, where should I make these CVS? I can use a simple word document or I can use a fancy CV template with a picture?

When creating your CV, it's important to consider readability, professionalism, and the potential challenges of getting through Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS). Here are the pros and cons of simple Word documents versus fancy CV templates with pictures:

**Simple Word Document CV:**
Pros:
- ATS-Friendly: Plain text formats without complicated designs are easily parsed by ATS software.
- Focus on Content: The emphasis is on your experiences, skills, and achievements without distractions from design elements.
- Easy to Edit: Simple formats are straightforward to adjust when tailoring your CV for different job applications.

Cons:
- Less Visually Distinctive: Without design elements, your CV won't stand out as much visually in a pile of applications.
  
**Fancy CV Template with a Picture:**
Pros:
- Aesthetically Pleasing: A well-designed CV can catch the eye and make a strong first impression.
- Personal Touch: Including a picture might help humanize your application and offer a personal connection.

Cons:
- ATS Challenges: Many ATS software have difficulty with images and complex layouts, which could strip out critical information from your CV.
- Potential Bias: Including a picture or other personal information can open the door to unconscious biases in the hiring process.
- Restrictive Editing: Templates with heavy design elements can be harder to edit and may not always adapt well when information changes.

**Best Practice Recommendations:**

- ATS Compatibility: Stick with a simple, clean, Word document format to ensure ATS compatibility. You can still use slight variations in font weight (bold, italics) and size to create visual hierarchy.
- Skip the Photo: In most business and tech industries, especially in Ireland and the UK, it's standard practice not to include a photo on your CV unless specifically requested by the employer or if it's customary in your field, such as for some public-facing roles.
- Professional Design: If you prefer to have some design elements, opt for minimalist templates that prioritize clarity and ATS-friendliness. There are many such templates available, including those from LinkedIn or other professional job-search platforms.

Remember, content is king. A well-written CV with a focus on your achievements, tailored to the job description, will generally have a stronger impact than a visually fancy CV that may not be read properly by an ATS or may distract from your qualifications.

What about a basic word cv using F pattern and bolded headings? maybe that is best for me

Using a basic Word CV with a clear 'F' pattern and bolded headings is indeed an excellent approach for several reasons:

1. **Readability**: Many studies on web usability suggest that people tend to scan documents in an 'F' pattern, first reading horizontally across the top part (forming the top bar of the 'F'), then down a bit on the left side, and then another horizontal scan (forming the bottom bar of the 'F'). This pattern applies to CV reading as well, so organizing your content in a way that suits this pattern can help ensure that the most important information catches the reader's eye.

2. **Clarity and Structure**: Bolded headings help structure your CV into clear sections (e.g., Objective/Summary, Education, Work Experience, Skills), making it easy for hiring managers and Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) to quickly find relevant information.

3. **ATS-Friendly**: A basic Word document without graphics, images, or complicated formatting is most likely to be successfully parsed by ATS, ensuring that your content is accurately read and accounted for when your CV is screened.

Here are some tips for creating your CV with these principles in mind:

- **Top Third**: Place your most compelling information in the upper third of your CV—the part that's guaranteed to get read first in an 'F' pattern. This includes your name, contact information, professional summary or objective, and any key qualifications or achievements.

- **Work Experience**: List your experience in reverse-chronological order, starting with the most recent. Bold each job title and use bullets for responsibilities and achievements to make them easily scannable.

- **Education**: Similar to the work experience section, bold your degree and the name of the institution, followed by any relevant details such as coursework, projects, or honors.

- **Skills**: Create a skills section with bolded categories, if relevant (e.g., "Technical Skills," "Languages," "Soft Skills"), and list the skills underneath in bullet or comma-separated format.

- **Consistency**: Maintain consistent formatting throughout your CV, including the style and size of your headers, the layout of your sections, and the fonts you use for body text.

- **White Space**: Use white space effectively to prevent your CV from looking too dense and to guide the reader's eye along the 'F' pattern.

- **Tailoring**: Remember to adapt your CV for each specific role you apply for, ensuring the content reflects the requirements and preferences of the job.

A basic but well-structured Word CV with conscious use of the 'F' pattern and bolded headings is a strong approach to presenting your qualifications effectively. It balances the need to pass ATS screenings with creating an easy-to-read document for recruiters and hiring managers.
